**Woodstock** is the shire [town](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town") ([county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat")[[3]](#cite_note-GR6-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)) of [Windsor County, Vermont](/wiki/Windsor_County,_Vermont "Windsor County, Vermont"), United States. As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_Census "2020 United States Census"), the town population was 3,005.[[5]](#cite_note-Census_2020-5) It includes the villages of [Woodstock](/wiki/Woodstock_(village),_Vermont "Woodstock (village), Vermont"), [South Woodstock](/wiki/South_Woodstock,_Vermont "South Woodstock, Vermont"), [Taftsville](/wiki/Taftsville_Historic_District "Taftsville Historic District"), and [West Woodstock](/wiki/West_Woodstock,_Vermont "West Woodstock, Vermont").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woodstock,_Vermont&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Chartered by New Hampshire Governor [Benning Wentworth](/wiki/Benning_Wentworth "Benning Wentworth") on July 10, 1761, the town was a [New Hampshire grant](/wiki/New_Hampshire_Grants "New Hampshire Grants") to David Page and 61 others. It was named after [Woodstock](/wiki/Woodstock,_Oxfordshire "Woodstock, Oxfordshire") in [Oxfordshire](/wiki/Oxfordshire "Oxfordshire"), England, as a homage to both [Blenheim Palace](/wiki/Blenheim_Palace "Blenheim Palace") and its owner, [George Spencer, 4th Duke of Marlborough](/wiki/George_Spencer,_4th_Duke_of_Marlborough "George Spencer, 4th Duke of Marlborough"). The town was first settled in 1768 by James Sanderson and his family.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 1776, [Joab Hoisington](/wiki/Joab_Hoisington "Joab Hoisington") built a [gristmill](/wiki/Gristmill "Gristmill"), followed by a [sawmill](/wiki/Sawmill "Sawmill"), on the south branch of the [Ottauquechee River](/wiki/Ottauquechee_River "Ottauquechee River").[[7]](#cite_note-books.google.com-7) The town was incorporated in 1837.[[8]](#cite_note-Woodstock,_Vermont-8)

Although the [Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") slowed settlement, Woodstock developed rapidly once the war ended in 1783. The [Vermont General Assembly](/wiki/Vermont_General_Assembly "Vermont General Assembly") met here in 1807 before moving the next year to the new capital at [Montpelier](/wiki/Montpelier,_Vermont "Montpelier, Vermont"). Waterfalls in the Ottauquechee River provided water power to operate mills. Factories made [scythes](/wiki/Scythe "Scythe") and axes, [carding machines](/wiki/Carding "Carding"), and woolens. There was a machine shop and gunsmith shop. Manufacturers also produced furniture, wooden wares, window sashes and blinds. Carriages, horse harnesses, saddles, luggage trunks and leather goods were also manufactured. By 1859, the population was 3,041.[[7]](#cite_note-books.google.com-7) The [Woodstock Railroad](/wiki/Woodstock_Railroad "Woodstock Railroad") opened to [White River Junction](/wiki/White_River_Junction,_Vermont "White River Junction, Vermont") on September 29, 1875, carrying freight and tourists. The Woodstock Inn opened in 1892.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") helped the town grow prosperous. The economy is now largely driven by [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism"). Woodstock has the 20th highest [per-capita income of Vermont towns](/wiki/Vermont_locations_by_per_capita_income "Vermont locations by per capita income") as reported by the United States Census, and a high percentage of homes owned by non-residents. The town's central square, called the Green, is bordered by restored late [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture"), [Federal Style](/wiki/Federal_architecture "Federal architecture"), and [Greek Revival](/wiki/Greek_Revival "Greek Revival") houses. The cost of real estate in the district adjoining the Green is among the highest in the state. The seasonal presence of wealthy second-home owners from cities such as [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") and [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") has contributed to the town's economic vitality and livelihood, while at the same time diminished its accessibility to native Vermonters.

The town maintains a free community wi-fi internet service that covers most of the village of Woodstock, dubbed "Wireless Woodstock".[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Layout and design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woodstock,_Vermont&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Layout and design")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Post_Office_in_Woodstock%2C_Vermont.png/250px-Post_Office_in_Woodstock%2C_Vermont.png)](/wiki/File:Post_Office_in_Woodstock,_Vermont.png)

US Post Office

In his *City Life: Urban Expectations in a New World*, Canadian author and architect [Witold Rybczynski](/wiki/Witold_Rybczynski "Witold Rybczynski") extensively analyzes the layout of the town and the informal and unwritten rules which determined it. According to Rybczynski:

> The overall plan seems to have been dictated by the site: a narrow, flat valley hemmed in by the sweeping curve of the Ottauqueechee River on one side and a small creek on the other. The green was laid out lengthwise on the narrow peninsula between the river and the creek, allowing for many plots to have rear gardens running down to the riverbank. ...
> The builders of Woodstock were aware that important buildings needed important sites. The Episcopalian church is at the head of the green, the Methodist farther down, and the Congregationalist church artfully closes the vista of Pleasant Street where it dead-ends into Elm Street. ... The pride of place, on the green, is shared by private homes on one side, and the courthouse and the Eagle Hotel on the other. Stores, banks, the post office and other businesses are located on two streets adjacent to but not actually on the green. This is a subtle sort of urban design, but it is design, design that proceeds not from a predetermined master plan, but from the process of building itself. A rough framework is established, with individual builders adapting as they come along. If Parisian planning in the grand manner can be likened to carefully scored symphonic music, the New England town is like ... very restrained jazz. ... [L]ike jazz, it involves improvisation, and as in jazz, this does not mean that the result is accidental or that there are no rules.[[11]](#cite_note-citylife-11)

The author goes on to explicate some of the informal rules, such as that buildings stand close to the sidewalk, in the case of businesses, or ten to fourteen feet behind for homes; that plots are generally deep and narrow, keeping street frontages roughly equivalent; commercial buildings stand side by side, with only important buildings with a public function—the library or courthouse, for instance—being free-standing objects. Rybczynsk points out that there is no zoning in Woodstock, and "buildings with different functions sat—and still sit today—side by side on the same streets", with practical exceptions such as the slaughterhouse and the gasworks.[[11]](#cite_note-citylife-11)

The [Rockefellers](/wiki/Rockefeller_family "Rockefeller family") have had an enormous impact on the overall character of the town as it exists today. They helped preserve the 19th century architecture and the rural feel.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the late 1960s they tore down the landmark Woodstock Inn, a Victorian treasure reconstructed in 1892 from the old Eagle Hotel, which served as a center point for the town, and built a charming neo-colonial edifice farther back from the street.[[12]](#cite_note-12) [Laurance](/wiki/Laurance_Rockefeller "Laurance Rockefeller") and [Mary French Rockefeller](/wiki/Mary_French_Rockefeller "Mary French Rockefeller") also had the village's power lines buried underground. To protect their ridgeline views, the town adopted an ordinance creating a Scenic Ridgeline District in order to protect the aesthetics and the views of the town. It was updated in 2007.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Woodstock was named "The Prettiest Small Town in America" by the *[Ladies Home Journal](/wiki/Ladies_Home_Journal "Ladies Home Journal")* magazine,[[14]](#cite_note-14) and in 2011, North and South Park Street and one block of Elm Street won an award for great streetscape by the [American Planning Association](/wiki/American_Planning_Association "American Planning Association")'s "Great Places in America" program. APA looks at street form and composition, street character and personality and the overall street environment and sustainable practices.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woodstock,_Vermont&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the town has a total area of 44.6 square miles (115.6 km2), of which 44.4 square miles (114.9 km2) is land and 0.27 square miles (0.7 km2), or 0.63%, is water.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The [Ottauquechee River](/wiki/Ottauquechee_River "Ottauquechee River") flows through the town.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Woodstock is crossed by [U.S. Route 4](/wiki/U.S._Route_4_in_Vermont "U.S. Route 4 in Vermont"), [Vermont Route 12](/wiki/Vermont_Route_12 "Vermont Route 12") and [Vermont Route 106](/wiki/Vermont_Route_106 "Vermont Route 106"). [Interstate 89](/wiki/Interstate_89 "Interstate 89") does not pass through the town, it is served by exit 1 in nearby [Quechee](/wiki/Quechee,_Vermont "Quechee, Vermont"). It is bordered by the town of [Pomfret](/wiki/Pomfret,_Vermont "Pomfret, Vermont") to the north, [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Vermont "Hartford, Vermont") to the northeast, [Hartland](/wiki/Hartland,_Vermont "Hartland, Vermont") to the east, [Reading](/wiki/Reading,_Vermont "Reading, Vermont") to the south, and [Bridgewater](/wiki/Bridgewater,_Vermont "Bridgewater, Vermont") to the west.

Woodstock is a three-hour drive from [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") and is 250 miles (400 km) away from [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"). It is easily accessible via car or plane to [Rutland](/wiki/Rutland_(city),_Vermont "Rutland (city), Vermont") or [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon,_New_Hampshire "Lebanon, New Hampshire") Airports. [Vermont Translines](/wiki/Vermont_Translines "Vermont Translines") operates a daily intercity bus route between Rutland and Lebanon, stopping in Woodstock along the way. The closest regular public transportation hubs are in [White River Junction](/wiki/White_River_Junction,_Vermont "White River Junction, Vermont") (12 miles (19 km) east) and Rutland (48 miles (77 km) west).

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woodstock,_Vermont&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

This [climatic](/wiki/Climate "Climate") region is typified by large seasonal temperature differences, with warm to hot (and often humid) summers and cold (sometimes severely cold) winters. According to the [Köppen Climate Classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Classification "Köppen Climate Classification") system, Woodstock has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate"), abbreviated "Dfb" on climate maps.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[1]](#cite_note-GR2-1) of 2010, there were 3,048 people, 1,388 households, and 877 families residing in the town. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 72.6 people per square mile (28.0/km2). There were 1,775 housing units at an average density of 39.9 per square mile (15.4/km2). The racial makeup of the town was 98.08% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 0.40% [Black](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)") or [African American](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.22% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 0.62% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.25% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 0.43% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race were 0.80% of the population.

There were 1,388 households, out of which 26.4% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 52.7% were couples living together and joined in either [marriage](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") or [civil union](/wiki/Civil_Union "Civil Union"), 8.1% had a female householder with no husband present, and 36.8% were non-families. 29.7% of all households were made up of individuals, and 11.3% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.24 and the average family size was 2.79.

In the town, the population was spread out, with 20.7% under the age of 18, 4.9% from 18 to 24, 23.9% from 25 to 44, 31.7% from 45 to 64, and 18.8% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 45 years. For every 100 females, there were 94.0 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 90.6 males.

The median income for a household in the town was $47,143, and the median income for a family was $57,330. Males had a median income of $33,229 versus $26,769 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the town was $28,326. About 4.3% of families and 6.4% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 8.3% of those under age 18 and 3.7% of those age 65 or over.

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woodstock,_Vermont&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

### Annual cultural events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woodstock,_Vermont&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Annual cultural events")]

* The annual Bookstock literary festival is held in June on and around the central village green and features regional, national and international authors and poets.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Past speakers include [Julia Alvarez](/wiki/Julia_Alvarez "Julia Alvarez"), [Richard Blanco](/wiki/Richard_Blanco "Richard Blanco"), [Billy Collins](/wiki/Billy_Collins "Billy Collins"), [Ed Koren](/wiki/Ed_Koren "Ed Koren"), [Sy Montgomery](/wiki/Sy_Montgomery "Sy Montgomery"), [Valerie Plame](/wiki/Valerie_Plame "Valerie Plame"), [Richard Russo](/wiki/Richard_Russo "Richard Russo"), [Ocean Vuong](/wiki/Ocean_Vuong "Ocean Vuong").[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* The annual Harvest Weekend at the Billings Farm and Museum is held in October and includes a husking bee, barn dance, and 19th century harvest activities.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* The annual Wassail Weekend is held in early December.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woodstock,_Vermont&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Tourism")]

The Billings Farm & Museum is a local tourist attraction. The land and farmhouse were owned by [Laurance Rockefeller](/wiki/Laurance_Rockefeller "Laurance Rockefeller") and his wife Mary French Rockefeller. The farm and museum include an operating dairy farm and a restored 1890 farm house.[[25]](#cite_note-Vermont_Standard-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

[F. H. Gillingham & Sons](/wiki/F._H._Gillingham_%26_Sons "F. H. Gillingham & Sons"), a [general store](/wiki/General_store "General store") located in its original building at 16 Elm Street, is another local tourist attraction. The store was established in 1886 by Frank Henry Gillingham.[[27]](#cite_note-F.H._Gillingham_&_Sons-27)

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woodstock,_Vermont&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

The [Marsh-Billings-Rockefeller National Historical Park](/wiki/Marsh-Billings-Rockefeller_National_Historical_Park "Marsh-Billings-Rockefeller National Historical Park") is located in Woodstock, and is the only unit of the [United States National Park System](/wiki/List_of_areas_in_the_United_States_National_Park_System "List of areas in the United States National Park System") in Vermont (except for the [Appalachian Trail](/wiki/Appalachian_Trail "Appalachian Trail")). The park preserves the site where Frederick Billings established a managed forest and a progressive dairy farm.[[25]](#cite_note-Vermont_Standard-25)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woodstock,_Vermont&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Education")]
